calendar.matters:
Notice Of Motion And Motion To Enforce Settlement Agreement
Off Calendar Jan-27-2022 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances; Patricia Medina appeared by zoom for Christopher Wong (petitioner).
Ross Madden and Paul Melbostad appeared by zoom for and with Carole Wong by zoom.

Mr. Melbostad's argument as to why this matter should be heard by Judge Wiss is rejected. The parties presented oral argument to support their positions regarding the issue of whether or not there was a mutual mistake of material facts as to how title of the property was held which delayed the process of Carole closing on the loan. Upon conclusion of argument, the court confirmed its tentative ruling. The Motion to Enforce Settlement Agreement is granted (see tentative ruling for details).

Judge: Ross C. Moody. Clerk: Rosie Noguera. Deputy: H. Singh. Not reported.

ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Status On Readiness Of Trial
Off Calendar Oct-26-2020 Off Calendar

Petition: (1) To Determine Validity Of Purported Trust And Will And To Impose Constructive Trust, (2
Off Calendar Jun-29-2020 Continued To Dec-07-20 At 09:30 A.M. In Dept 206
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances: Alan Sparer appeared by telephone for Christopher Wong.
Ross Madden appeared by telephone for Carole Wong.

The Court stated that it is its understanding that the Parties anticipate fact and expert discovery to be concluded by October and suggested setting a trial date. The Petition to Determine Validity of Purported Trust and Will and to Impose Constructive Trust is set for trial in the Department of the Presiding Judge (206) on December 7, 2020 at 9:30 a.m., (order for the same signed in open court). Status on readiness of trial is set for October 26, 2020 at 9:00 a.m., in Dept. 204. The Parties are to provide a joint status report.

Judge: Mary E. Wiss. Clerk: Rosie Noguera. Deputy: K. Cabasares. Not reported.

Petition: (1) To Determine Validity Of Purported Trust And Will And To Impose Constructive Trust, (2
Off Calendar Apr-02-2018 Continued To Jun-11-18 At 09:00 A.M. In Dept 204

Carole S. Wongs Motion For Order Quashing Subpoenas Duces Tecum And For Expenses Of The Motion
Off Calendar Mar-15-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances: Ross Madden for petitioner Carole Wong; Marc Haber for objector Christopher Wong.

Mr. Madden stated that there were six records subpenas served, for which two were clearly relevant and therefore no motion to quash had been filed. As to the remaining subpenas, the sought documents as to every bank account held by petitioner, including many personal accounts which were completely unrelated to the decedent in any way. He argued that the subpenas were overly broad in scope and unduly impinged upon his clients constitutionally protected right of privacy over her personal finances. He opined that case law cited with the petition established a requirement for precise tailoring of document requests seeking disclosure of personal financial information, unlike the subpenas before the Court. He stated that Carole Wong would be happy to share relevant information, and that the objector should depose Ms. Wong to ask about specific transactions causing concern. In response to an inquiry from the Court regarding the parties' connection with Michael Mah, Mr. Madden responded that Mr. Mah had been a long-time caregiver for the decedent, who was afriend both of Carole Wong and of the decedent.
Mr. Haber responded that his client did not know about all the accounts, only the ones for which information was given to them. Without access to all the accounts controlled by Ms. Wong, the objector would have to guess where money from the decedent's accounts and peoperties went. He said that records provided to date revealed transfers involving tens of thousands of dollars. He contended that he would need the bank and other records prior to taking the deposition of Ms. Wong in order to properly prepare for the deposition. He contended that restricting the records to be produced to records from 2011 to date was too narrow a time frame, as Ms. Wong managed and controlled the property from 2006 onwards, and the earlier records from 2006-2011 were likewise relevant and subject to discovery.
The Court stated that the information sought from Carole Wong was relevant, but that he would require a protective order to be in place prior to requiring the documents to be produced. He granted the motion to quash to the extent the subpenas sought records of Michael Mah. He directed the parties to confer regarding the terms of a protective order. The motion to quash subpenas was granted in part and denied in part, subject to review of order. The Court directed Mr. Haber to prepare an appropriate order and to submit it to Mr. Madden for approval as to form.

Judge: John K. Stewart, Clerk: Craig Blackstone. Bailiff: Deputy V. Jung. (Not reported)
